summary: Hasidism (Hebrew Chasidut, from chasid, 'pious one') is the popular mystical revival movement that arose in the mid-eighteenth century in Podolia and Volhynia (then southeastern Poland, now Ukraine), traced by its adherents to Israel ben Eliezer, the Baal Shem Tov (c. 1698-1760). Drawing on Lurianic Kabbalah but recasting it for a mass following, it stresses the divine presence in all things, joyful and fervent prayer with mystical intention (kavvanah) and cleaving to God (devekut), and the leadership of a charismatic holy man, the tzaddik or rebbe, whose court and dynasty gather a community around him. Spread by disciples of the Maggid of Mezeritch into a network of distinct courts (Chabad-Lubavitch, Breslov, Satmar, Ger, and others), it was opposed by the Mitnagdim, the circle of the Vilna Gaon, who issued bans against it.
description:
Hasidism (Chasidut, the abstract noun from chasid, a 'pious' or devout person) is the name of the mystical revival movement that emerged among the Jews of Podolia and Volhynia, in the southeastern lands of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth (today largely in Ukraine), in the middle decades of the eighteenth century. In the movement's own account it originates with Israel ben Eliezer (c. 1698-1760), known as the Baal Shem Tov, 'Master of the Good Name' (acronym the Besht), a healer and mystic whose circle of associates and disciples carried and reshaped his teaching. The movement drew heavily on the Kabbalah, and especially on the Lurianic theosophy of the sparks of divine light scattered through creation and their gathering-up (tikkun), but recast this inherited mysticism into a form addressed to a broad following rather than to an elite of adepts.

Several emphases are commonly identified as characteristic of Hasidic piety, and are given here as the movement's own attributed teaching rather than as claims the editor affirms. The first is the divine immanence, the presence of God filling and animating all things, so that the created world is a veil of, and not a barrier to, the divine; the worshipper is counselled to keep God 'before me always'. The second is devekut, 'cleaving' or attachment to God, cultivated as a continuous state and not only in set moments of prayer. The third is the manner of worship: fervent, embodied prayer offered with kavvanah (mystical intention or concentration) and, above all, with joy (simcha), the movement teaching that God is to be served in gladness and that sadness and melancholy are obstacles to be resisted. The fourth is the figure of the tzaddik (the 'righteous one') or rebbe, the charismatic holy leader held to have attained an exceptional closeness to God and to mediate divine abundance to his followers; around such a leader gathered a court (a 'hoyf'), and leadership was in many cases transmitted dynastically, giving rise to enduring Hasidic courts.

As a movement Hasidism was diffused not chiefly by the Besht himself, who committed almost nothing to writing, but by later leaders, above all Dov Ber, the Maggid ('Preacher') of Mezeritch, and the wide circle of disciples he trained and dispatched, from whom the major Hasidic courts descend, among them Chabad-Lubavitch (founded by Shneur Zalman of Liady), Breslov (of Nachman of Breslov), and later dynasties such as Satmar and Ger. The rapid spread of Hasidism provoked determined opposition from other rabbinic authorities, the Mitnagdim ('opponents'), associated above all with the Vilna Gaon (Elijah ben Solomon) and the rabbinic establishment of Lithuania, who objected to what they saw as innovations in prayer and law, the exaltation of the tzaddik, and the movement's enthusiasm, and who issued bans of excommunication (herem) against the Hasidim in 1772 and after. This conflict between Hasidim and Mitnagdim is recorded here as a historical dispute, with the concerns of both sides attributed to them and neither adjudicated. The relation of the movement to the historical Besht is itself a subject of scholarship: modern historians (Gershom Scholem, and more recently Moshe Rosman and Immanuel Etkes) distinguish the Besht recoverable from contemporary documents and his own circle from the later hagiographic image of him as founder, and debate how far Hasidism as a mass movement should be traced to his intention at all; these scholarly reconstructions are recorded alongside the movement's own account without the editor ruling between them.
editorial_notes: This entry describes Hasidism on Judaism's own terms (neutrality #1, #4, #6). Its characteristic emphases (divine immanence, devekut, joyful prayer with kavvanah, the tzaddik/rebbe and his court) are stated throughout as the movement's own attributed teaching ('given here as the movement's own attributed teaching', 'on this account'), described and not affirmed or denied by the editor. The Hasidim-Mitnagdim conflict, including the bans (herem) of 1772 and after, is reported as a HISTORICAL DISPUTE with the concerns of BOTH sides attributed and NOT adjudicated (neutrality #1-#2): the Vilna Gaon's circle and the Hasidim are each given their objections without the editor ruling who was right. The scholarly quest for the historical Besht (Scholem, then Rosman and Etkes) versus the later hagiographic image of him as founder is distinguished and attributed (neutrality #1, #5), and the entry does not treat the movement's foundation-account as settled fact. Sources triangulate three lenses per neutrality #8-#9: (a) academic historians of Hasidism (Scholem, Major Trends in Jewish Mysticism, ninth lecture 'Hasidism: The Latest Phase'; Ada Rapoport-Albert, ed., Hasidism Reappraised); (b) the tradition's own primary text, open and auditable on Sefaria, the Tzava'at ha-Rivash ('Testament of the Rivash', the collected directives of the Besht and the Maggid), read line-by-line and quoted verbatim on joy, simplicity of service, and constant attachment to God; and (c) contemporary Jewish scholars who are also its leading historians (Moshe Rosman, Founder of Hasidism; Immanuel Etkes, The Besht). No encyclopedias (no Encyclopaedia Judaica, no Jewish Encyclopedia, no Chabad.org, no My Jewish Learning, no YIVO Encyclopedia), no Wikipedia, no tertiary sources. CONFIDENCE / HONESTY: the Tzava'at ha-Rivash on Sefaria is rated HIGH because sections were read line-by-line and the load-bearing lines on joyful service, wholehearted simplicity (temimut), and keeping God 'before me always' were confirmed verbatim; the English there is a CC-BY translation (Markel, 2020) over a public-domain Hebrew (Warsaw, 1913), so the entry is verifiable and verified-eligible on this open primary. The secondary works (Scholem, Rapoport-Albert, Rosman, Etkes) are medium, verified to exist and be on-topic via publisher and library records, not read line-by-line; Scholem, Rosman, and Etkes carry the same ratings as in the baal-shem-tov entry in this batch. Relation targets: baal-shem-tov and kabbalah are same-batch / existing targets, isaac-luria and rabbinic-judaism exist; maggid-of-mezeritch, vilna-gaon, mitnagdim, tzaddik, devekut, and shivchei-ha-besht are dangling forward references to not-yet-created entities.

period.note: Hasidism arose in Podolia and Volhynia in the middle decades of the eighteenth century; c. 1740 is an approximate scholarly marker for the period in which Israel ben Eliezer, the Baal Shem Tov (c. 1698-1760), became active as a mystic and healer and gathered the circle from which the movement grew, not a precise foundation date (the very question of when and by whom Hasidism was 'founded' is itself debated in the scholarship). The movement spread as a mass phenomenon under the Maggid of Mezeritch and his disciples from the 1760s-1770s and remains active today in numerous courts, so no end date is given.

relations.note: The movement traces its origin to Israel ben Eliezer, the Baal Shem Tov (c. 1698-1760), in its own account and in the standard scholarship; the scholarly debate over how far he 'founded' Hasidism as a movement is noted in the description and editorial notes. The baal-shem-tov entry is authored in the same batch.

Dov Ber, the Maggid ('Preacher') of Mezeritch (d. 1772), was the principal organizer and disseminator of Hasidism after the Besht, training the circle of disciples from whom the major Hasidic courts descend. Dangling forward reference to a not-yet-created figure entity.

Hasidism drew heavily on the Kabbalah, and especially on Lurianic theosophy (the divine sparks scattered through creation and their gathering-up, tikkun), recasting it for a mass following. The kabbalah entry exists (authored in a prior batch).

The Lurianic Kabbalah of Isaac Luria (the Ari, 1534-1572), with its sparks, kelipot, and tikkun and its emphasis on mystical intention (kavvanah), is a principal source on which Hasidic teaching draws. The isaac-luria entry exists.

Hasidism teaches devekut, 'cleaving' or continuous attachment to God, cultivated as an ongoing state and not only in set prayer; stated as the movement's attributed teaching. Dangling forward reference to a not-yet-created concept.

The tzaddik (the 'righteous one') or rebbe, the charismatic holy leader held to mediate divine abundance to his followers and around whom a court and dynasty form, is a defining institution of Hasidism; stated as the movement's attributed teaching. Dangling forward reference to a not-yet-created concept.

The Mitnagdim ('opponents'), the circle of the Vilna Gaon and the Lithuanian rabbinic establishment, opposed Hasidism and issued bans of excommunication (herem) from 1772; reported as a historical dispute with both sides attributed and not adjudicated (neutrality #1-#2). Dangling forward reference to a not-yet-created entity.

The Shivchei ha-Besht ('In Praise of the Baal Shem Tov', first printed 1814) is the earliest collection of hagiographic legends about the Besht and a principal (though hagiographic, not documentary) source for the movement's foundation-narrative. Dangling forward reference to a not-yet-created work entity.

Hasidism developed within rabbinic Judaism, its leaders and followers being observant of halakha and steeped in Talmud; the movement's disputes with the Mitnagdim were internal to the rabbinic world. The rabbinic-judaism entry exists.

sources.citation: Tzava'at ha-Rivash (Tzava'at HaRivash, the 'Testament of the Rivash', i.e. of Rabbi Israel Baal Shem), open Hebrew and English text on Sefaria; a collection of the directives and teachings of the Baal Shem Tov and his disciple the Maggid of Mezeritch. Cited as the openly-auditable primary text carrying the movement's spine, read line-by-line: section 1 opens 'Rabbi Yisrael Baal Shem, peace be upon him, taught us to be wholehearted in our service of HaShem, blessed is He, and to serve Him with simplicity' (temimut); section 44 teaches, against the melancholy induced by the evil inclination, 'On the contrary, I will serve Him with joy' (serving God in simcha rather than sadness); and section 2 develops the continuous awareness of God, 'I have set HaShem before me always', as 'a very lofty level'. These lines were read and verified verbatim as the load-bearing text for divine immanence, joyful service, and devekut.
sources.license: English: CC-BY (The Way of the Baal Shem Tov, adapted into English by Rabbi Amiram Markel and Yehudah Shimon Markel, 2020, via neirot.com). Hebrew: public domain (Warsaw, 1913). Openly auditable; read line-by-line.

sources.citation: Scholem, Gershom. Major Trends in Jewish Mysticism. New York: Schocken Books, 1941 (many later printings; 3rd rev. ed. 1954). The foundational modern academic study of Jewish mysticism, whose ninth and final lecture, 'Hasidism: The Latest Phase', is a standard scholarly treatment of the movement, its relation to Lurianic Kabbalah, its ideal of devekut and joy, and the neutralization of messianism; cited as the academic-historian lens on Hasidism. Verified to exist and be on-topic (Schocken / Penguin Random House record; Internet Archive), not read line-by-line.

Rapoport-Albert, Ada, ed. Hasidism Reappraised. London and Portland: The Littman Library of Jewish Civilization, 1996. xxiv + 514 pp. A collection of essays by the leading scholars of the field re-examining the history, theology, social structure, and leadership (including her own 'Hasidism after 1772: Structural Continuity and Change') of the movement; cited as the academic lens on the historiography of Hasidism, the tzaddik and the court, and the movement's development after the Besht. Verified to exist and be on-topic (Littman Library record; AJS Review notice), not read line-by-line.
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference (publisher / library record).

sources.citation: Rosman, Moshe. Founder of Hasidism: A Quest for the Historical Ba'al Shem Tov. Contraversions 5. Berkeley: University of California Press, 1996 (reprinted, with a new introduction, Oxford: Littman Library, 2013). A contemporary Jewish scholar's archival reconstruction of the Besht and the origins of Hasidism, drawing on Polish and Hebrew documents to distinguish the historical figure and his circle from the later hagiographic image; cited as the contemporary-Jewish-scholarship and historical lens on the movement's origins and the quest for the historical Besht. Verified to exist and be on-topic (University of California Press record; 1996 National Jewish Book Award), not read line-by-line.

Etkes, Immanuel. The Besht: Magician, Mystic, and Leader. Translated by Saadya Sternberg. The Tauber Institute Series for the Study of European Jewry. Waltham: Brandeis University Press, 2005. A contemporary Jewish scholar's study of the Baal Shem Tov as ba'al shem (magician-healer), mystic, and leader, and of the early Hasidic circle; cited as the contemporary-Jewish-scholarship and historical lens on the Besht's roles and the movement's beginnings, and on the debate over whether the Besht intended to found a movement. Verified to exist and be on-topic (Brandeis University Press / University Press of New England record; History of Religions review), not read line-by-line.
